title: AAR — SQLite connection leak surfacing as `disk I/O error`
scope: Production logs (2026-06-07) showed sqlite3.OperationalError: disk I/O error 500s across unrelated routes.

rationale.item: One failure fires on a bare PRAGMA immediately after connect(), and another on the first SELECT of a freshly opened connection — the classic signature of the process having run out of file descriptors: SQLITE_IOERR is what SQLite raises when it cannot open the file handles it needs.

The errors hitting multiple routes at once (the almanac uses its own sqlite3.connect, which already closes in a finally) confirmed the almanac was a victim, not the source — something else was exhausting the process-wide fd budget.

Root cause: Python's sqlite3 context manager only manages the transaction — __exit__ commits or rolls back and then deliberately leaves the connection open. So every db.fetchone/fetchall/execute — and the ~30 fantasy write paths that did conn = db.get_conn() with no close at all — leaked the connection's file descriptors until CPython's GC happened to finalize the object.

actions.description: get_conn now builds its connection from a sqlite3.Connection subclass, _ManagedConnection, whose __exit__ runs the normal commit/rollback and then closes; covers every with get_conn() call site (db.fetchone/fetchall/execute/executemany, plus sim, trades, streaks, injuries, init backfills, and the fantasy fdb helpers); fdb.get_conn opts in via the same factory=

The ~29 bare conn = db.get_conn() fantasy write sites (which never used with at all) got an explicit conn.close() after their final commit(); for settle paths calling wallet.credit() afterward, the close lands before the credit

risk_posture: Two changes, both about closing connections; no query or schema logic touched.
Transaction semantics are preserved — a with get_conn() as conn: block still commits on success / rolls back on error; it just also closes now.
Audited all 25 with get_conn() sites first to confirm none reuse the connection after the block (which auto-close would break).
Left as-is to keep the diff a minimal, reviewable set of one-line additions rather than re-indenting every multi-line SQL literal.

validation_claims.claim: Reproduced in a sandbox: OLD pattern (with conn:, no close): fd delta over 200 conns = +401 (~2 fds each, leaked); NEW db.fetchone (fixed): fd delta over 200 calls = +1 (flat)
validation_claims.check_method: Run a loop of connection opens under each pattern and count /proc/self/fd
python -m py_compile on the changed files

validation_claims.claim: Per-file conn = db.get_conn() count == conn.close() count (4/4, 3/3, …).
validation_claims.check_method: grep-count get_conn vs close per file

validation_claims.claim: Runtime fd test (temp DB via O27V2_DB_PATH): the subclass's __exit__ closes the connection, and 500 iterations of fetchall + fetchone + execute produce a net fd delta of 0 (was +1500-ish before).
validation_claims.check_method: Run the same fd-accounting loop against a temp DB

validation_claims.claim: Not run: the Flask route smoke test and pytest — flask/pytest are absent in this sandbox (expected per CLAUDE.md).
validation_claims.check_method: Run pytest and the route smoke test in an environment with flask installed

non_actions.item: try/finally-wrapping the exception path on the bare write sites
non_actions.reason: if one of those rare writes raises before its close(), that single connection still leaks until GC. The steady-state leak (every successful read/write) is the one that exhausted fds, and that is closed. Left as-is to keep the diff a minimal, reviewable set of one-line additions rather than re-indenting every multi-line SQL literal.

non_actions.item: Addressing potential genuine disk pressure (a -wal that never checkpoints, a full volume)
non_actions.reason: that is a separate ops issue; watch the volume's free space and WAL size after this lands. The fd leak was, however, sufficient on its own to produce exactly the observed errors.

residual_risks: The exception path on the bare write sites is not try/finally-wrapped: if one of those rare writes raises before its close(), that single connection still leaks until GC.
If the live box also has genuine disk pressure, that is a separate ops issue; watch the volume's free space and WAL size after this lands.
section_headers_original: AAR — SQLite connection leak surfacing as `disk I/O error`
unmapped_content: Symptom detail: GET /almanac/players/lad_achille_gonzález.html -> 500 at o27/almanac/loader.py:123 and GET /college/game/4848 -> 500 at o27v2/db.py:76 (PRAGMA synchronous = NORMAL), both sqlite3.OperationalError: disk I/O error. Each page render fans out into many calls; fantasy fdb connections are doubly expensive: they ATTACH the sim DB, so ~2 db handles + WAL/SHM each. Under sustained load the open-file count drifted up against the process RLIMIT_NOFILE, after which any new connection — even one only running a PRAGMA — failed to open its files.
parser_notes: No explicit document date line; '2026-06-07' is the date of the production logs (the incident), not stated as the document/completion date, so record is undated per instructions. Non-action 'left as-is' categorized declined_by_owner is debatable; it was the author's minimal-diff choice.
